perturbation.original_text: ●IMedicor will make its records available for audit purposes at any time by USA MCO during regular business hours at the headquarters of iMedicor.
perturbation.changed_text: ●IMedicor will allow USA MCO to request access to its records for potential audit purposes during what iMedicor considers regular business hours.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states IMedicor will make its records available for audit at any time during business hours. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating 'potential audit purposes' and 'what iMedicor considers regular business hours.' This makes the audit process discretionary and subject to iMedicor's interpretation, potentially hindering USA MCO's ability to verify revenue sharing and other financial details.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the spirit and intent of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act, which emphasizes transparency and accuracy in financial reporting. By making the audit process ambiguous and subject to IMedicor's discretion, it undermines the ability of USA MCO to independently verify the financial information related to revenue sharing, which could impact financial statements. SOX requires companies to maintain accurate and reliable financial records and to provide reasonable access to these records for audit purposes. The vague language in the modified clause could be interpreted as restricting access or providing misleading information, which is in violation of SOX's core principles of corporate governance and financial transparency. SOX Act requires public companies to implement and maintain internal controls to ensure the accuracy and reliability of their financial reporting, including revenue recognition. Vague audit clauses hinder monitoring these controls.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces ambiguity regarding audit access, potentially hindering USA MCO's ability to verify financial information, undermining the spirit of financial transparency emphasized by SOX. The original contract clearly mandates records to be available at any time during business hours whereas the modified text introduces language that gives more leeway to IMedicor. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 7262, discusses management's responsibility for internal controls and the rules required by the Commission which indicates the importance of internal controls in annual reports and the responsibility of management for them, which could be undermined by the ambiguous wording.

perturbation.original_text: All revenue sharing will be reconciled monthly and funds distributed by the 20th of each month by wire transfer for all net collected revenues for the previous month (net of returns and refunds, if any).
perturbation.changed_text: Revenue sharing may be reconciled on a monthly basis, and funds will be distributed around the 20th of each month through a mutually agreed-upon method for all net collected revenues, subject to deductions.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear obligation for monthly reconciliation and payment by the 20th. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'will be reconciled' with 'may be reconciled,' 'by wire transfer' with 'through a mutually agreed-upon method,' and making deductions less specific. This weakens the obligation for timely and consistent revenue sharing. 'Subject to deductions' is particularly problematic because it does not specify what deductions are allowed, or how these are to be calculated.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although this contract is governed by New York law, USA MCO is a Texas corporation. Therefore, laws related to accounting and prompt payment have potential relevance. By making the timing and method of payment vague, this contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts under Texas law. Section 2.309 addresses performance or acceptance under reservation of rights. By introducing ambiguity into payment reconciliation and methods, the modified text could allow for disputes about what constitutes 'net collected revenues' and allowable deductions, potentially breaching the implied covenant of good faith. While not a direct violation of a specific prompt payment statute, the ambiguity allows for interpretation that could delay or reduce payments unfairly.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.306. OUTPUT, REQUIREMENTS AND EXCLUSIVE DEALINGS. (a) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith, except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (b) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.307. DELIVERY IN SINGLE LOT OR SEVERAL LOTS. Unless otherwise agreed all goods called for by a contract for sale must be tendered in a single delivery and payment is due only on such tender but where the circumstances give either party the right to make or demand delivery in lots the price if it can be apportioned may be demanded for each lot. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.308. ABSENCE OF SPECIFIED PLACE FOR DELIVERY. Unless otherwise agreed (1) the place for delivery of goods is the seller's place of business or if he has none his residence; but (2) in a contract for sale of identified goods which to the knowledge of the parties at the time of contracting are in some other place, that place is the place for their delivery; and (3) documents of title may be delivered through customary banking channels. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.309. ABSENCE OF SPECIFIC TIME PROVISIONS; NOTICE OF TERMINATION. (a) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this chapter or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (b) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (c) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Acts 1967, 60th Leg., p. 2343, ch. 785, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1967. Sec. 2.310. OPEN TIME FOR PAYMENT OR RUNNING OF CREDIT; AUTHORITY TO SHIP UNDER RESERVATION. Unless otherwise agreed (1) payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the goods even though the place of shipment is the place of delivery; and (2) if the seller is authorized to send the goods he may ship them under reservation, and may tender the documents of title, but the buyer may inspect the goods after their arrival before payment is due unless such inspection is inconsistent with the terms of the contract (Section 2.513 ); and (3) if delivery is authorized and made by way of documents of title otherwise than by Subdivision (2) then payment is due regardless of where the goods are to be received: (A) at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive delivery of the tangible documents; or (B) at the time the buyer is to receive delivery of the electronic documents and at the seller's place of business or if none, the seller's residence; and (4) where the seller is required or authorized to ship the goods on credit the credit period runs from the time of shipment but post-dating the invoice or delaying its dispatch will correspondingly delay the starting of the credit period. perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding payment reconciliation, methods, and allowable deductions, potentially contradicting the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Texas law. The original text sets a clear obligation for monthly reconciliation and payment by the 20th, while the modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'will be reconciled' with 'may be reconciled,' 'by wire transfer' with 'through a mutually agreed-upon method,' and making deductions less specific. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 2.309(a) states that "The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this chapter or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time." By making the timing and method of payment vague, the changed text allows for interpretations that could delay or reduce payments unfairly, therefore, the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: IMedicor shall provide access to the iMedicor system, training and customer support as required.
perturbation.changed_text: IMedicor will endeavor to provide access to the iMedicor system, along with training and customer support, as it deems feasible.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states a clear obligation for IMedicor to provide access, training, and customer support. The modified text uses 'will endeavor,' 'along with', and 'as it deems feasible,' weakening the obligation and making it discretionary. This introduces uncertainty about the level of support USA MCO providers will receive.
perturbation.contradicted_law: HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996)
perturbation.law_explanation: Given that iMedicor is described as a HIPAA compliant system, the availability of training and support is crucial for maintaining compliance. HIPAA requires covered entities and business associates to implement security awareness and training programs for their workforce. By making training discretionary ('as it deems feasible'), IMedicor potentially fails to ensure that USA MCO providers are adequately trained on HIPAA security requirements when using the iMedicor system. This increased the risk of HIPAA violations, because if USA MCO providers are not trained adequately on HIPAA security measures, they might inadvertently violate patient privacy regulations, leading to penalties and legal liabilities.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: the provisions of the Security Rule. 73 Documentation. A regulated entity must maintain documentation required for written policies and procedures implemented to comply with the Security Rule and actions, activities, or assessments required by the Security Rule to be documented until six years after the later of: 1) the date of the document’s creation or 2) the date the document was last in effect. 74 Availability. A regulated entity must make the written policies and procedures it implements to comply with the Security Rule and its documentation of required actions, activities, and assessments available to those persons responsible for implementing the procedures described in the documentation. 75 Updates. A regulated entity must periodically review and update its documentation in response to environmental or organizational changes that affect the security of ePHI. 76 Endnotes [1]Subtitle F of title II of HIPAA (Pub. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding IMedicor's obligation to provide training and support, which are crucial for HIPAA compliance. The original text clearly states that iMedicor 'shall provide' access, training, and support. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating iMedicor 'will endeavor to provide' these services 'as it deems feasible.' This shift from a clear obligation to a discretionary one creates a contradiction because, as the Law Explanation notes, HIPAA requires covered entities and business associates to implement security awareness and training programs. By making training discretionary, IMedicor potentially fails to ensure adequate training on HIPAA security requirements, increasing the risk of HIPAA violations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the necessity of maintaining documentation related to policies and procedures implemented to comply with the Security Rule, suggesting a need for consistent and reliable training programs to ensure compliance, something undermined by the changed text.
